title: About

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text: Longitudinal data from a clinical trial of the anti-epileptic drug progabide. Each of 59 patients was observed over 4 follow-up periods, with seizure counts recorded at each visit, alongside baseline seizure count and age. Commonly used to teach Poisson regression for count data with repeated measurements on the same subject.

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Thall and Vail (1990), distributed in the MASS R package
URL: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/MASS/
License: Available via the MASS package (part of base R distribution)

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Dataset Characteristics

Observations: 236
Variables: 9
Missingness present: FALSE
Longitudinal: TRUE
Hierarchical: TRUE

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Variable Dictionary








Variable
Type
Description




TRUE
count
Number of seizures during the follow-up period


trt
categorical
Treatment group: placebo or progabide


base
count
Baseline seizure count (8 weeks prior to trial)


age
continuous
Patient age in years


V4
binary
Indicator for the 4th (final) follow-up period


subject
categorical
Patient identifier (1-59), repeated across 4 rows each


period
ordinal
Follow-up period number (1 to 4)


lbase
continuous
Log-transformed, centered baseline seizure count


lage
continuous
Log-transformed, centered age

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit a Poisson regression model for count outcomes
Recognize the need to account for repeated measurements per subject
Compare naive Poisson models to overdispersion-corrected alternatives

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Model seizure counts as a function of treatment and baseline rate, ignoring then accounting for repeated measures

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating each of the 4 visits per patient as an independent observation

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("epilepsy-seizures")

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text: Passenger-level data on the 1912 Titanic disaster, including age, sex, ticket class, and survival outcome. Widely used to introduce logistic regression, classification, and discussions of confounding.

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Kaggle Titanic dataset, derived from Encyclopedia Titanica
URL: https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic
License: CC0-1.0

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Dataset Characteristics

Observations: 891
Variables: 12
Missingness present: TRUE
Longitudinal: FALSE
Hierarchical: FALSE

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Variable Dictionary



Variable
Type
Description




PassengerId
continuous
Unique passenger identifier


Survived
binary
0 = died, 1 = survived


Pclass
ordinal
Ticket class: 1 = first, 2 = second, 3 = third


Name
text
Passenger name


Sex
categorical
Passenger sex


Age
continuous
Age in years (some values missing)


SibSp
count
Number of siblings/spouses aboard


Parch
count
Number of parents/children aboard


Ticket
categorical
Ticket number


Fare
continuous
Passenger fare


Cabin
categorical
Cabin number (mostly missing)


Embarked
categorical
Port of embarkation: C, Q, or S

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit and interpret a logistic regression model
Recognize confounding by passenger class and sex

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare crude vs. adjusted survival odds by sex

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating fare as a clean linear predictor without checking skew

title: Titanic Passenger Survival
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("titanic-survival")

title: StatsData Hub
text:
Most dataset repositories are built for finding data. StatsData Hub is built for teaching with data — helping instructors, TAs, and students find datasets aligned with specific statistical methods, teaching concepts, and course levels.



Browse datasets by statistical method, teaching concept, variable type, course level, and discipline
View detailed dataset pages with variable dictionaries, suggested learning objectives, classroom activities, and example analyses
Load datasets directly in R using the companion statsdata package





Browse the dataset collection
Install the R package
Contribute a dataset
